title: Install Godot 4 & Tour the Editor
html:
Get Godot 4 running

Godot is a free, open-source engine that ships as a single executable with no installer required. Go to godotengine.org/download and grab the Godot 4.x Standard build for your OS. The .NET / C# build is a separate download you only need if you plan to write C#; this course uses GDScript, so the Standard build is correct.

  • Windows/Linux: unzip and double-click the executable. It is fully portable, so put it anywhere.
  • macOS: unzip, drag Godot.app to Applications. The first launch may need a right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.
One file, no install.
Godot does not write to system folders. To upgrade, you just download a newer executable. Keep old versions around — projects are tied to engine versions.

The Project Manager

The first window is the Project Manager, not the editor. Click New, give the project a name like DragDemo, choose an empty folder, and leave the renderer on Forward+ for desktop (or Compatibility if you also target web/mobile). Click Create & Edit.

The four main docks

The editor layout is consistent across every Godot project. Learn these four regions and you can navigate anything.

RegionWhat it does
Scene dock (top-left)The tree of nodes in the currently open scene
FileSystem dock (bottom-left)Every file on disk under res:// (your project folder)
Viewport (center)The 2D/3D editing canvas; tabs 2D, 3D, Script, AssetLib across the top
Inspector (right)Every editable property of the selected node
res:// vs user://
res:// is your read-only project (bundled into exports). user:// is a writable per-user folder for save files. Never try to write into res:// at runtime.

Running a project

The play buttons are top-right. F5 (Run Project) launches the main scene; F6 (Run Current Scene) launches whatever scene tab is open. A brand-new project has no main scene, so F5 will prompt you to pick one. Use F6 while building individual scenes.

Build the drag demo

Create a root node: in the Scene dock click Other Node, choose Node2D (a plain 2D node). Add a child: click the +, add a Sprite2D. With the Sprite2D selected, in the Inspector set its Texture to Godot's built-in icon by dragging icon.svg from the FileSystem dock onto the Texture slot. To attach behaviour, select the Sprite2D and click the Attach Script icon (scroll-with-plus) above the Scene tree.

draggable.gd
extends Sprite2D var dragging := false func _input(event: InputEvent) -> void: # Start/stop drag on left mouse button if event is InputEventMouseButton and event.button_index == MOUSE_BUTTON_LEFT: if event.pressed: # Only grab if the click is over our texture var rect := Rect2(global_position - texture.get_size() * 0.5, texture.get_size()) dragging = rect.has_point(event.position) else: dragging = false # Follow the mouse while dragging elif event is InputEventMouseMotion and dragging: global_position = event.position

Press F6. A window opens with the icon; click and drag it. You have a running interactive scene.

🎮 Build This: Draggable Sprite

A window showing a sprite you can pick up and move with the mouse.

  1. New project, root Node2D, child Sprite2D with the icon texture.
  2. Attach draggable.gd above and paste the script.
  3. Press F6, set this as the current scene, and drag the sprite around.
  4. Bonus: set the window title in Project Settings → Display → Window.

title: Nodes, Scenes & the Scene Tree
html:
Everything is a node

A node is the atomic unit in Godot. Each node does one job: Sprite2D draws a texture, AudioStreamPlayer plays sound, Timer counts down, CollisionShape2D defines a hitbox. You build behaviour by composing many small nodes into a tree, not by writing one giant class.

Scenes are reusable trees

A scene is a saved tree of nodes (a .tscn file). The key insight: there is no real difference between a "scene" and a "game object." A coin, an enemy, a bullet, a whole level, and the entire game are all just scenes. A scene can be instanced (dropped) inside another scene as many times as you want.

The composition rule.
If a subtree of nodes is something you will reuse or spawn at runtime, make it its own scene. One scene = one reusable thing.

Build a Coin scene

Create a new scene: Scene → New Scene. For the root, choose Node2D and rename it Coin (double-click the node name). Add a child Sprite2D with the icon texture, and tint it gold in the Inspector under Modulate. Save with Ctrl/Cmd+S as coin.tscn. Notice it now appears as a file in the FileSystem dock with a distinctive scene icon.

The owner and ready order

When a scene loads, nodes enter the tree depth-first, children before parents finish. Godot calls _ready() on a node only after all its children are ready — so a parent's _ready() can safely touch its children. This is why _ready() is the place to grab node references.

Instance the Coin into a grid

Make another new scene with a Node2D root named CoinField. To place a coin, click the chain/link icon in the Scene dock ("Instantiate Child Scene") and pick coin.tscn. It appears with a special instance icon — you can move it but its internals are locked to the original. Edit coin.tscn and every instance updates. That is the power of scene instancing.

Rather than placing dozens by hand, spawn the grid in code on the parent:

coin_field.gd
extends Node2D # preload bakes the scene into the script at compile time const CoinScene := preload("res://coin.tscn") const COLS := 8 const ROWS := 5 const SPACING := 80 func _ready() -> void: for row in ROWS: for col in COLS: var coin := CoinScene.instantiate() # make a new copy coin.position = Vector2(col, row) * SPACING + Vector2(64, 64) add_child(coin) # attach it to the tree
preload vs load.
preload() runs when the script is parsed (faster, errors caught early) and only takes a constant path. load() runs at the moment it is called and accepts a variable path — use it for assets chosen at runtime.
instantiate() does not add to the tree.
A freshly instantiated scene exists in memory but is invisible and gets no _process calls until you add_child() it. Forgetting add_child is the #1 "why is nothing showing" bug.

Run F6: a tidy 8×5 grid of coins. Because each coin is an instance, changing the gold tint in coin.tscn recolors all 40 at once.

🎮 Build This: Coin Grid

A grid of instanced coin scenes spawned from a tileable scene.

  1. Build coin.tscn: Node2D root + tinted Sprite2D.
  2. Build coin_field.tscn and attach coin_field.gd.
  3. Spawn an 8×5 grid in _ready() with the loop above.
  4. Bonus: scale each coin slightly differently with coin.scale = Vector2.ONE * randf_range(0.8, 1.2).

title: GDScript Basics & the _process Loop
html:
GDScript in 90 seconds

GDScript is a Python-like language built for Godot. It uses indentation for blocks, optional static typing, and integrates directly with the node tree. A script always begins with extends, naming the node class it attaches to.

syntax.gd
extends Node2D var speed := 200.0 # := infers float type var lives: int = 3 # explicit type annotation const MAX_LIVES := 5 # constant, cannot change func add_score(points: int) -> int: return points * 10 # typed return value func _ready() -> void: var v := Vector2(1, 0) # built-in 2D vector type print("start at ", position, " v=", v.normalized())
Type your variables.
The := and : Type forms enable autocompletion and catch errors before you run. Untyped code works but loses these safety nets — prefer typed GDScript.

The two lifecycle callbacks you need now

CallbackWhenUse for
_ready()Once, after the node and its children enter the treeSetup, caching references, initial state
_process(delta)Every rendered frameMovement, animation, anything visual

There is also _physics_process(delta), called at a fixed rate (60 Hz by default) — use it for physics and CharacterBody2D movement. For now, _process is fine.

Why delta matters

delta is the seconds elapsed since the previous frame. A 60 Hz monitor gives delta ≈ 0.0166; a 144 Hz monitor gives ≈ 0.0069. If you move position.x += 5 per frame, the sprite travels at different speeds on different machines. Multiply by delta and movement becomes framerate-independent: position.x += speed * delta always means "speed pixels per second."

Multiply motion by delta.
Any per-frame change to position, rotation, or a timer should be scaled by delta. Forgetting this is the most common motion bug for beginners.

Build the bouncing square

New scene, Node2D root, child Sprite2D (the icon, tinted). Attach this to the root:

bouncer.gd
extends Node2D @onready var sprite: Sprite2D = $Sprite2D # $ finds a child by name var velocity := Vector2(220, 160) # pixels per second var bounds := Vector2(1152, 648) # default window size var half: Vector2 func _ready() -> void: half = sprite.texture.get_size() * sprite.scale * 0.5 sprite.position = bounds * 0.5 # start centered func _process(delta: float) -> void: sprite.position += velocity * delta # frame-independent move # Reverse direction at each edge if sprite.position.x - half.x < 0 or sprite.position.x + half.x > bounds.x: velocity.x = -velocity.x if sprite.position.y - half.y < 0 or sprite.position.y + half.y > bounds.y: velocity.y = -velocity.y
@onready and $.
$Sprite2D is shorthand for get_node("Sprite2D"). The @onready annotation defers the assignment until _ready(), when children exist — assigning at variable-declaration time would crash because the tree is not built yet.
Sticky edges.
If you only flip velocity without checking direction, a fast object can re-trigger the flip next frame and jitter at the wall. Robust code also clamps position back inside, or checks that velocity points outward before flipping.

Run F6 — the square ricochets endlessly around the window at a constant real-world speed.

🎮 Build This: Bouncing Square

A sprite that drifts and bounces off all four window edges.

  1. Scene with Node2D + Sprite2D; attach bouncer.gd.
  2. Give it a velocity and move by velocity * delta in _process.
  3. Flip velocity.x / velocity.y at the matching edges.
  4. Bonus: randomize the start direction with velocity = Vector2.from_angle(randf() * TAU) * 250.

title: Signals & the Input Map
html:
Signals: nodes that announce, not command

A signal is Godot's built-in observer pattern. A node emits a signal when something happens; other nodes connect to it and react. This decouples code: a Ball does not need to know a Scoreboard exists — it just shouts "I scored!" and whoever cares listens.

ball.gd (declaring a signal)
extends Area2D signal scored(who: String) # custom signal with one argument func _on_goal_reached(side: String) -> void: scored.emit(side) # Godot 4: signal.emit(args)

Connect signals in code or in the editor. In the editor, select the emitting node, open the Node → Signals dock, double-click a signal, and pick the receiving node — Godot generates a callback stub. In code:

main.gd (connecting)
func _ready() -> void: $Ball.scored.connect(_on_ball_scored) func _on_ball_scored(who: String) -> void: print(who, " scored!")
Built-in signals everywhere.
Buttons emit pressed, timers emit timeout, Area2D emits body_entered. You connect to them the same way as custom ones.

The Input Map: never hard-code keys

Open Project → Project Settings → Input Map. Add named actions like left_up, left_down, right_up, right_down. For each, click + and press the physical key to bind it (e.g. W/S for the left paddle, Up/Down for the right). Now your code asks about intent, not hardware:

paddle.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var up_action := "left_up" # set per paddle in Inspector @export var down_action := "left_down" var speed := 500.0 func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: var dir := Input.get_axis(up_action, down_action) # -1, 0, or 1 velocity.y = dir * speed move_and_slide() position.y = clampf(position.y, 40, 608) # keep on screen
Actions, not scancodes.
Input-map actions let you rebind keys, support gamepads, and add multiple bindings per action — all without touching gameplay code. @export exposes a variable in the Inspector so the same paddle script serves both players.

Build Pong

Scene structure: a Node2D root named Pong holding two CharacterBody2D paddles (each with a CollisionShape2D rectangle and a Sprite2D/ColorRect), one CharacterBody2D ball, and a Label for the score. The ball uses move_and_collide and flips velocity on hit:

pong_ball.gd
extends CharacterBody2D signal scored(side: String) var speed := 420.0 func _ready() -> void: velocity = Vector2(1, randf_range(-0.4, 0.4)).normalized() * speed func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: var hit := move_and_collide(velocity * delta) if hit: velocity = velocity.bounce(hit.get_normal()) # reflect off paddle/wall velocity = velocity.normalized() * speed # Off the left/right edge = a point if position.x < 0: scored.emit("right"); reset() elif position.x > 1152: scored.emit("left"); reset() func reset() -> void: position = Vector2(576, 324) velocity = Vector2([-1, 1].pick_random(), randf_range(-0.4, 0.4)).normalized() * speed
pong.gd (root)
extends Node2D var left := 0 var right := 0 func _ready() -> void: $Ball.scored.connect(_on_scored) func _on_scored(side: String) -> void: if side == "left": left += 1 else: right += 1 $Score.text = "%d - %d" % [left, right]
🎮 Build This: Pong

Two-player Pong with input-map controls and a signal-driven score.

  1. Add input actions for both paddles in the Input Map.
  2. Build paddles from CharacterBody2D; set each paddle's @export actions.
  3. Ball reflects with velocity.bounce() and emits scored.
  4. Root connects scored and updates a Label.

title: Sprites, AnimatedSprite2D & Atlases
html:
Importing pixel art the right way

Drop a PNG into your project folder and it appears in the FileSystem dock. Before using it, select it and open the Import tab (top-left, next to Scene). For crisp pixel art, set Filter to Nearest (or turn off filtering) and Mipmaps off, then click Reimport. Linear filtering blurs pixels — Nearest keeps hard edges.

Project-wide default.
Set Project Settings → Rendering → Textures → Canvas Textures → Default Texture Filter to Nearest so every imported sprite is crisp without per-file fiddling.

Sprite2D regions vs AtlasTexture

A sprite sheet packs many frames into one image. To show just one frame on a Sprite2D, enable Region in the Inspector and set the rect, or use Hframes/Vframes to split the sheet into a grid and pick a Frame index. For shared sub-images across many nodes, create an AtlasTexture resource pointing at the sheet with a region — it behaves like its own texture.

AnimatedSprite2D & SpriteFrames

For frame-based animation, use AnimatedSprite2D. Add the node, then in the Inspector create a new SpriteFrames resource on its Sprite Frames property. Click it to open the SpriteFrames editor at the bottom. There you:

  1. Rename the default animation (e.g. spin) and set its FPS.
  2. Click Add frames from a Sprite Sheet, pick your PNG, set the grid (e.g. 4×1), and select frames.
  3. Toggle Loop on for continuous animations.
animated_ball.gd
extends AnimatedSprite2D func _ready() -> void: play("spin") # start the looping animation func flash() -> void: play("hit") # one-shot animation on a hit await animation_finished # wait for the built-in signal play("spin") # return to idle
await a signal.
await animation_finished pauses the function until that signal fires, then resumes — clean sequencing without timers or state machines.

Build Breakout (visuals + structure)

Compose three scenes: brick.tscn (a ColorRect or Sprite2D), paddle.tscn (input-driven like Pong), and ball.tscn (an AnimatedSprite2D spinning). The root spawns a wall of bricks in a grid, identical to the coin-grid loop from Lesson 2:

breakout.gd
extends Node2D const Brick := preload("res://brick.tscn") const COLS := 10 const ROWS := 6 var bricks_left := 0 func _ready() -> void: for row in ROWS: for col in COLS: var b := Brick.instantiate() b.position = Vector2(col * 100 + 80, row * 40 + 80) # color rows differently for arcade feel b.modulate = Color.from_hsv(float(row) / ROWS, 0.8, 1.0) b.cleared.connect(_on_brick_cleared) # signal stub for now add_child(b) bricks_left += 1 func _on_brick_cleared() -> void: bricks_left -= 1 if bricks_left == 0: print("Level clear!")
brick.gd (collision stubbed)
extends Node2D signal cleared func hit() -> void: # wired to real collision next lesson cleared.emit() queue_free() # remove this brick from the tree
queue_free, not free.
Call queue_free() to delete a node safely at the end of the frame. Calling free() mid-signal or mid-physics can crash with a freed-object error.

For now, trigger hit() manually (e.g. on a key press hitting a random brick) to confirm the wall clears and the level-clear message fires. Real ball-brick collision arrives next lesson with physics.

🎮 Build This: Breakout (visuals)

An arcade brick wall with an animated ball and an input-driven paddle.

  1. Import a 4-frame ball sheet; build AnimatedSprite2D with a looping spin.
  2. Make brick.tscn with a cleared signal and queue_free().
  3. Spawn a 10×6 colored wall from breakout.gd.
  4. Bonus: count bricks_left and print "Level clear!" at zero.

title: 2D Physics: CharacterBody2D, Area2D & Layers
html:
Three bodies, three jobs

NodeUse it for
CharacterBody2DPlayer/enemy you move by code with move_and_slide()
RigidBody2DObjects the physics engine fully simulates (gravity, bounces)
Area2DNon-solid trigger zones — detect overlap, no collision response

Every body needs a child CollisionShape2D (or CollisionPolygon2D) with an actual shape resource assigned, or it does nothing.

Moving a CharacterBody2D

CharacterBody2D exposes a velocity property; you set it, call move_and_slide(), and Godot moves the body, sliding along walls and stopping at collisions. Do this in _physics_process for stable timing.

player.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var speed := 300.0 const Bullet := preload("res://bullet.tscn") func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: # 2D 8-way movement from input-map actions var dir := Input.get_vector("left", "right", "up", "down") velocity = dir * speed move_and_slide() if Input.is_action_just_pressed("fire"): shoot() func shoot() -> void: var b := Bullet.instantiate() b.global_position = global_position # aim toward the mouse b.direction = (get_global_mouse_position() - global_position).normalized() get_tree().current_scene.add_child(b) # add to scene, not to player
Spawn bullets on the scene, not the shooter.
If you add_child(b) to the player, the bullet inherits the player's transform and moves with them. Add it to get_tree().current_scene (or a dedicated bullets node) so it lives in world space.

Area2D bullets and overlap detection

Bullets are Area2D nodes: they fly through the world and report overlaps without bouncing off things. Connect the built-in area_entered / body_entered signals.

bullet.gd
extends Area2D var direction := Vector2.RIGHT var speed := 700.0 func _ready() -> void: area_entered.connect(_on_area_entered) # auto-despawn after 2s so bullets do not leak get_tree().create_timer(2.0).timeout.connect(queue_free) func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: position += direction * speed * delta func _on_area_entered(area: Area2D) -> void: if area.is_in_group("enemies"): area.queue_free() # destroy enemy queue_free() # destroy bullet

Layers and masks: who collides with whom

Each physics node has a collision layer ("what I am") and a collision mask ("what I scan for"). Two nodes interact only if one's mask includes the other's layer. Name your layers in Project Settings → Layer Names → 2D Physics to stay sane.

ObjectLayerMask
PlayerPlayerEnemy, Walls
EnemyEnemyPlayer, PlayerBullet
Player bulletPlayerBulletEnemy
Layer = identity, Mask = interest.
Set the player bullet's mask to only the Enemy layer and it physically ignores walls and the player — no code checks needed. Combine with add_to_group("enemies") for clean code-side filtering.
Monitorable matters.
For two Area2Ds to detect each other, the target must have Monitorable on and the detector must have Monitoring on. If overlaps never fire, check these two toggles first.

Make enemies simple Area2D nodes added to the enemies group. The bullet's area_entered then deletes both on contact. Player-enemy contact can damage the player via the player's own Area2D hitbox.

🎮 Build This: Top-Down Shooter Core

A player who walks 8 ways, shoots at the mouse, and kills enemies on hit.

  1. Player CharacterBody2D with Input.get_vector movement and move_and_slide().
  2. bullet.tscn as an Area2D aimed at the mouse, self-despawning after 2s.
  3. Enemies as Area2D in group enemies; configure layers/masks so bullets hit only enemies.
  4. On area_entered, free both bullet and enemy.

title: Tilemaps & Level Building
html:
TileMapLayer in Godot 4

Godot 4.3+ replaced the old monolithic TileMap node with TileMapLayer: each layer (background, ground, decoration) is its own node, which is cleaner to manage. Add a TileMapLayer node; its core property is a TileSet resource that defines the available tiles.

Building a TileSet

  1. Select the TileMapLayer and create a new TileSet on its Tile Set property.
  2. At the bottom, the TileSet panel opens. Set the tile size (e.g. 16×16 or 32×32) to match your art.
  3. Drag your tile-sheet PNG into the TileSet panel as an atlas source. Godot offers to auto-create tiles for the whole sheet — accept it.
Match tile size to art.
If your sheet is 16px tiles but the TileSet is set to 32, tiles will be cropped or doubled. Set the size before importing the atlas.

Adding collision to tiles

Solid ground needs collision data baked into the tiles. In the TileSet panel:

  1. Add a Physics Layer in the TileSet's Inspector (this defines which collision layer the tiles occupy).
  2. Switch the bottom panel to Select, choose the ground tile, and in the tile's properties draw the collision polygon — usually the full square (a one-click "reset to full rect" is available).
  3. Repeat for each solid tile. Decorative tiles get no collision polygon.
Collision lives in the TileSet, not the map.
You define a tile's shape once; every painted instance of that tile is solid automatically. This is why tilemaps are so efficient for level geometry.

Painting the level

With the TileMapLayer selected, the bottom panel switches to the TileMap editor. Pick a tile from the palette and left-click-drag in the 2D viewport to paint. Right-click erases. Use the rectangle and bucket tools for large fills. Build a floor and a couple of platforms.

A platformer player

The player is a CharacterBody2D with gravity. Godot 4 provides get_gravity() from project settings, and is_on_floor() works because the tilemap supplies collision.

platformer_player.gd
extends CharacterBody2D @export var speed := 250.0 @export var jump_velocity := -450.0 var gravity := ProjectSettings.get_setting("physics/2d/default_gravity") func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: # apply gravity while airborne if not is_on_floor(): velocity.y += gravity * delta # jump only when grounded if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump") and is_on_floor(): velocity.y = jump_velocity # horizontal run var dir := Input.get_axis("left", "right") if dir != 0: velocity.x = dir * speed $Sprite2D.flip_h = dir < 0 else: velocity.x = move_toward(velocity.x, 0, speed) # friction move_and_slide()
Up is negative Y.
In 2D, the Y axis points down, so a jump uses a negative velocity (-450) and gravity adds positive Y. This trips up everyone once.
is_on_floor stale data.
is_on_floor() reflects the last move_and_slide(). Always call your jump/gravity logic before move_and_slide() in the same frame, and let move_and_slide() update floor state for next frame.

Set the player's collision mask to include the tilemap's physics layer so they land on the ground. Run F6 and you can run and jump around a real, painted level. Add a few coins (Area2D, group coins) for the player to collect, reusing the overlap pattern from Lesson 6.

🎮 Build This: Platformer Level

A tilemap world with solid ground and a jumping CharacterBody2D player.

  1. Add a TileMapLayer, build a TileSet with a physics layer and per-tile collision.
  2. Paint a floor and two platforms.
  3. Add the CharacterBody2D player with gravity, run, and jump from the script above.
  4. Bonus: scatter Area2D coins and collect them on body_entered.

title: UI Control Nodes, Audio & Save Files
html:
Control nodes & anchors

UI in Godot uses Control nodes, not Node2D. They live under a CanvasLayer so they stay fixed on screen regardless of camera movement. The core layout tool is anchors: a Control's edges are pinned to fractions of its parent. Use the Layout menu in the toolbar ("Full Rect", "Center", "Top Wide") to set common presets instead of dragging by hand.

ControlPurpose
LabelDisplay text (score, title)
ButtonClickable; emits pressed
VBoxContainer / HBoxContainerAuto-arrange children in a column/row
MarginContainerAdd padding around a child
Let containers do layout.
Put buttons inside a VBoxContainer and they stack and resize automatically. Manually positioning UI breaks on different window sizes — containers and anchors do not.

A start menu and HUD

main_menu.gd
extends Control func _ready() -> void: $VBox/PlayButton.pressed.connect(_on_play) $VBox/QuitButton.pressed.connect(get_tree().quit) func _on_play() -> void: get_tree().change_scene_to_file("res://level.tscn")

The in-game HUD is a CanvasLayer containing a Label for the score, updated whenever the score changes (via a signal from the game logic).

Audio

Use AudioStreamPlayer for non-positional sound (music, UI clicks) and AudioStreamPlayer2D for sounds with a world position (panning). Assign a sound file to the Stream property and call play().

player.gd (audio bits)
@onready var jump_sfx: AudioStreamPlayer = $JumpSound @onready var coin_sfx: AudioStreamPlayer = $CoinSound func jump() -> void: velocity.y = jump_velocity jump_sfx.play() func collect_coin() -> void: coin_sfx.play()
Audio buses & looping music.
Mark a music file's import as Loop on (in the Import tab) so it repeats seamlessly. Route SFX and Music to separate audio buses (Audio panel) so a settings menu can mute or set volumes independently.

Saving a high score

Persist data to user:// — never res://. JSON is the simplest portable format. Read it back defensively in case the file does not exist yet.

save.gd (autoload singleton)
extends Node const PATH := "user://save.json" var high_score := 0 func _ready() -> void: load_game() func save_game() -> void: var f := FileAccess.open(PATH, FileAccess.WRITE) f.store_string(JSON.stringify({"high_score": high_score})) # f closes automatically when it goes out of scope func load_game() -> void: if not FileAccess.file_exists(PATH): return var f := FileAccess.open(PATH, FileAccess.READ) var data: Variant = JSON.parse_string(f.get_as_text()) if data is Dictionary: high_score = int(data.get("high_score", 0)) func try_set_high_score(score: int) -> bool: if score > high_score: high_score = score save_game() return true return false
Make Save an autoload.
In Project Settings → Autoload, register save.gd as Save. Now any script can call Save.high_score and Save.try_set_high_score(n) from anywhere — a global singleton that survives scene changes.
JSON parses floats.
JSON.parse_string returns numbers as floats, so an int saved as 5 comes back as 5.0. Wrap reads in int(...) to avoid subtle type bugs.
🎮 Build This: Platformer Shell

A start menu, score HUD, jump/coin sounds, and a persistent high score.

  1. Build main_menu.tscn with Play/Quit in a VBoxContainer; Play changes scene.
  2. Add a HUD CanvasLayer with a score Label updated on coin pickup.
  3. Add AudioStreamPlayer jump and coin sounds; call play() on each event.
  4. Register Save as an autoload; on level end, call Save.try_set_high_score(score).

title: Capstone: A Polished Platformer & Exporting
html:
Assemble the whole game

The capstone pulls every system together: the tilemap level (1.7), the CharacterBody2D player (1.6/1.7), Control-node menus and HUD (1.8), audio, and the Save autoload. Structure your scene flow with a small game manager.

SceneRole
main_menu.tscnTitle, Play, shows saved high score
level.tscnTilemap world, player, coins, HUD
game_over.tscnFinal score, "new high score!", Retry/Menu
level.gd
extends Node2D var score := 0 func _ready() -> void: for coin in get_tree().get_nodes_in_group("coins"): coin.collected.connect(_on_coin_collected) $HUD/Score.text = "Score: 0" $HUD/Best.text = "Best: %d" % Save.high_score func _on_coin_collected() -> void: score += 100 $HUD/Score.text = "Score: %d" % score $CoinSound.play() func finish_level() -> void: var is_best := Save.try_set_high_score(score) get_tree().change_scene_to_file("res://game_over.tscn")

Polish that matters

  • Camera follow: add a Camera2D as a child of the player and enable it; set limits so it stops at level edges.
  • Coyote time: allow a jump for ~0.1s after leaving a ledge using a small timer — it dramatically improves feel.
  • Particles / tween juice: a quick create_tween() scale-punch on coin pickup adds life.
  • Window settings: set the project name, icon, and a fixed/expand stretch mode in Project Settings → Display.
Set the main scene.
Project Settings → Application → Run → Main Scene = main_menu.tscn. Now F5 and exported builds boot into your menu.

Export templates

Before exporting, download the matching export templates: Editor → Manage Export Templates → Download and Install. These are the engine binaries Godot bundles into your game. They must match your editor version exactly.

Desktop export

Open Project → Export. Click Add… and choose Windows Desktop, Linux/X11, or macOS. Set an output path and click Export Project. For Windows, enable Embed PCK to ship a single .exe; otherwise you get an .exe plus a .pck data file that must stay together.

terminal — verify the build
$ ./MyPlatformer.x86_64 # Linux: run the exported binary Godot Engine v4.x.stable # the game window opens at the main menu

Web (HTML5) export

Add a Web export preset. Web requires the Compatibility renderer (Project Settings → Rendering → Renderer) — Forward+ will not run in browsers. Export to an empty folder; you get index.html, a .wasm, a .pck, and JS glue.

Web needs a server with the right headers.
Browsers block opening index.html via file://, and Godot's threaded web build requires cross-origin isolation headers (COOP/COEP). Use the editor's built-in Remote Debug → Run in Browser, or serve over HTTP with those headers set, or disable threads in the export preset.
terminal — quick local web test
$ python3 -m http.server 8000 --directory export/web # open http://localhost:8000 (note: no COOP/COEP — disable threads or use Godot's server)
Match templates to version.
The single most common export failure is a template/editor version mismatch after an engine upgrade. Re-download templates whenever you change Godot versions.
🎮 Build This: Capstone Platformer + Export

A complete small platformer with menus, HUD, audio, save, and shipped builds.

  1. Wire main_menu → level → game_over with change_scene_to_file; set the main scene.
  2. Show saved best on the menu; update it via Save.try_set_high_score at level end.
  3. Add a Camera2D follow, coin SFX/juice, and coyote-time jump.
  4. Install export templates; export a desktop build and run it.
  5. Switch to Compatibility renderer; export an HTML5 build and test it over HTTP.
